title: This Supernova Showed Up Five Times — and It Could Solve a Giant Space Mystery
summary: Imagine looking up at the night sky and seeing the same star in five different places at once. That's exactly what's happening with a supernova called SN Winny. A supernova is a huge explosion that happens when a star dies. This one is extra special — it's a superluminous supernova, which means it shines way brighter than normal. But the really wild part? Gravity from two galaxies in front of it is bending its light like a giant magnifying glass. This makes the supernova show up as five separate points of light! Scientists call this gravitational lensing. Because the light takes different paths around the galaxies, each image arrives at Earth at a slightly different time. By measuring those tiny time differences, astronomers can figure out something really important: how fast the universe is expanding. Right now, there's a big argument about this. Two main ways of measuring the expansion give different answers, and nobody knows why. This is called the Hubble tension. SN Winny could help solve it because it offers a brand-new, one-step method. The team spent six years searching for a supernova lined up perfectly with a lensing galaxy. The chance of finding one? Less than one in a million! Now they're watching SN Winny with telescopes around the world, hoping to finally crack the mystery of our growing universe.
why_it_matters: By solving this space mystery, we might understand how our universe began and what will happen to it billions of years from now.

keywords.explanation: A huge explosion that happens when a star dies.
Shining much brighter than a normal supernova.
When gravity from galaxies bends light like a giant magnifying glass.
Scientists who study stars and space.
The big argument between two ways of measuring how fast the universe is expanding.
Everything that exists, including all stars and galaxies.

questions.question: Why does SN Winny appear in five different places at once?
options: It is very close to Earth

questions.question: What is a superluminous supernova?
options: A star that is very small
A supernova that shines much brighter than normal
A supernova that lasts only a second
questions.correct_answer: A supernova that shines much brighter than normal

questions.question: What can astronomers measure by observing the different arrival times of SN Winny's light?
options: How fast the universe is expanding
questions.correct_answer: How fast the universe is expanding

questions.question: What is the Hubble tension?
options: An argument between two methods measuring universe's expansion
questions.correct_answer: An argument between two methods measuring universe's expansion

questions.question: How long did the team search to find a supernova lined up with a lensing galaxy?

background_read: Edwin Hubble (an American astronomer) discovered in 1929 that galaxies are moving apart, proving the universe is expanding.
Gravitational lensing was predicted by Albert Einstein (a famous scientist) long before anyone saw it happen.
Normally, a supernova fades after a few months, but SN Winny is still being watched by telescopes around the world.
Article_Structure: WHO: Astronomers and scientists studying a supernova.
WHAT: Supernova SN Winny appears as five points of light because of gravitational lensing.
WHERE: Seen from Earth through telescopes, with the supernova behind two galaxies.
WHY: To measure how fast the universe is expanding and solve the Hubble tension.

perspectives.description: Scientists are excited because SN Winny offers a new, direct way to measure the universe's growth, which could settle the Hubble tension.
Some people might worry that relying on one rare event could be risky, and it may take many more discoveries to be sure.
Astronomers understand that this is a lucky find, but they'll need many similar events to fully test the method.
